posts.name: Alex Offut
posts.cooked:
The Pilgrimage to visit MacDonald

Norah and I had last visited with Alex & Graeme about 2 or 3 years ago, and while we spent time with Alex when he was in Dallas last summer, we definitely wanted to make sure went back to visit them again to catch up.

We met Alex MacDonald at Graeme and Sarah’s cottage and walked over to the main house. Graeme was out on the tractor at the time, have just finished mowing the ground cover over the past week. We had noticed that many in the valley, including their neighbor’s and cousins, the Deterts, had long ago mowed and plowed under the ground cover, but Alex & Graeme said they had waited as they wanted to keep the moisture out of the soil. Anyhow, just the fact that Alex and Graeme put so much thought and energy into each little decision as it pertains to their vines and and their wine never ceases to impress.

For those that have walked their vineyard before, the route is familiar. We walked up the northern side (next to the creek) towards the border with the Detert vineyards, and then walked south along the western edge of their vineyards, next to the 20 year old vines. We walked over to the road that divides their plot from Mondavi, and actually took a slight detour over to look at Mondavi’s Old Monastery Block. The vines there are pretty old and gnarly, but what was interesting to hear was that most are not in the best health. No idea here, but not sure how much longer this block of 60+ year old vines will survive.

We then walked over to MacDonald’s 1/2 acre of 60 year vines and saw where Graeme had skipped a spot while mowing because there was a duck nest with about a dozen eggs in it at the base of a large vine! Again, the connection to the land and nature in the way they farm their land has a very nostalgic/romantic aspect to it. We then started walking back towards the house, past the almond trees (I grabbed a few raw almonds, but they weren’t quite ready!) that line the block of 40 year year old vines. They have finally put in rootstock to replace some vines that were removed a few years ago, so the vineyard was scattered with dozens of boxes of Almond Breeze that were being used to protect the new plantings! lol!

When we got back to the house Alex was kind enough to pour us a bottle of the soon to be released '14 MacDonald Cabernet. He said that he had decanted it about 4 hours by the time he poured. Needless to say the wine jumps out at you. Norah and I are constantly amazed at the complexity of MacDonald on the nose. Dark red fruits and balanced floral notes. The texture is just perfect. Tannins are well integrated and smooth. I definitely drank that fist pour way too fast! Thankfully Alex was more than happy to keep refiling our glasses. Their '14 Cab is going to be another special wine. Like all MacDonald cab, so incredibly approachable when young. I feel like this wine will definitely benefit from a few years of bottle age, but when the time comes it will really come into its own! So all of you who buy MacDonald, do yourself a favor and try to restrain yourselves when it arrives in the fall!

We were there about 3 hours when we had to leave as we were having dinner with Alex and Graeme later than night. I won’t spend much time on Dinner, but Alex and Graeme were so generous and surprised us at dinner with a bottle of their 2009 MacDonald Cabernet. As many know Alex and Graeme only made 1 barrel of the 2009 so this was incredibly generous of them to bring this bottle. We also drank a 2008 Next of Kyn and 2009 Promontory, and while the Next of Kyn was fantastic, the MacDonald was easily the best wine of the night. I had the 2009 MacDonald with Alex in Dallas last year when we tasted a full 5 year vertical (2008-2013) of MacDonald, and it was exactly as I remembered it. Definitely a wine that has come into its own. It will be fun to try it again at the 10 year vertical tasting we have planned for 2021!

As an aside, Alex and Graeme’s generosity in opening the bottle of 2009 has inspired me to reciprocate in a manner, and I will be auctioning off one of my bottles of 2009 MacDonald to benefit the Leukemia & Lymphoma Society. I will post more on this later today.

-Alex

posts.name: Barry Paul Price
posts.cooked: Sounds like a wonderful visit. Thank you for being so detailed in your description that I felt like I was walking through the vineyard this Thursday morning, rather than sitting here at my desk. lol.

posts.name: Brian Armston
posts.cooked:
Nice write up!

I had the pleasure of visiting with Alex back in FEB. We toured the grounds during fits of downpour rain, but were rewarded with a glimpse of the 2014 as well. As for the wine, you could tell it has serious complexity and will only benefit from additional bottle age. It was my first experience with MADCDONALD, and will for sure be buying the maximum allocated this Fall.

As for the people, its easy to support them. Alex was super genuine and provided some really interesting history on his family, the site, etc.

In an area where its not always easy to find quality folks, these guys seem to fit the bill.

Fan for Life…

posts.name: Alan Eden
posts.cooked:
Alex

How you rate Macdonald to the top two Napa wines, SE and Harlan ?
That sounds like an awesome experience, I think we are are envious. Thank you for sharing.

posts.name: Alex Offut
posts.cooked:
Alan Eden:

Alex

How you rate Macdonald to the top two Napa wines, SE and Harlan ?

Alan,

I haven’t ever had a bottle of SE. As for Harlan, I’ve only had a few. So I’m probably not the person to try comparing MacDonald to those guys. What I can say is that from a QPR perspective, I think MacDonald is no. 1.

I would be interested to hear from someone who has tried younger SE and Harlan and MacDonald and could speak to Alan’s question.

-Alex

posts.name: Justin Wittkopf
posts.cooked: Thanks for sharing your experience, Alex. Alex and Graem are the best. I’ve visited several times but still always enjoy reading the reports of others’ experiences. It brings back fond memories. Sounds like you had a great time.

posts.name: Bill Tex Landreth
posts.cooked:
Bdklein:
Alan Eden:

Alex

How you rate Macdonald to the top two Napa wines, SE and Harlan ?

Just curious. What are the criteria that SE and Harlan are the top 2 Napa wines? IMO SE is considered the “ultimate” Napa cult wine but what makes Harlan the other contender ?

I don’t see Screaming Eagle and Harlan as the top two either quantitatively or qualitatively. They are expensive and have tons of hype and fanboy following.

Having said that, MacDonald for me blows both of them away. I have had 15+ vintages of Harlan and two of SE for the record.

I would also put any number of other Napa cabs ahead of either.

Why not ?

Im a fan of Macdonald and also friends with Alex. My question was not attacking anything just a genuine curiousity of how Macdonald rates compares to the top Napa cabs ( even if only top by price )

I know you dont like my posts and complain im offensive but in this case i will defend my question as a perfectly reasonable non offensive question in a posting about high end Napa cabs. How are people like me supposed to learn which higher end Napa cabs are all about if we dont ask challenging questions.

So while i will apologize if im offensive in this case i feel its you who is being dimissive and somewhat arrogant

Oh puleeeeese. Just stop, just stop now. I’d rather argue about preferences between a Cezanne and Monet…

posts.name: Bill Tex Landreth
posts.cooked:
J a y H a c k:
Bill Tex Landreth:

. . . Harlan and two of SE . . .

I would also put any number of other Napa cabs ahead of either.

Do tell.

Since Harlan is the poster child for VA, basically any well made Napa Cab/Blend/Meritage qualifies for me.

As for Screaming Eagle, these easily are better at a fraction of the cost (I own bottles of all of them):

Spottswoode
Rivers Marie Panek
Maybach Materium
Older Beringer Private Reserve
Paradigm
Myriad Dr. Crane Elysium
Phelps Backus
Herb Lamb Estate
Anything Russell Bevan is pumping out
etc.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Agreed that their 2014 is great. After tasting 500+ 2014 California Cabernets between February and today, I can happily say it rates very highly indeed. The most graceful vintage of MacDonald to date.
